1	---
2	title: "Plugin Entry Points"
3	sidebarTitle: "Entry Points"
4	summary: "Reference for definePluginEntry, defineChannelPluginEntry, and defineSetupPluginEntry"
5	read_when:
6	  - You need the exact type signature of definePluginEntry or defineChannelPluginEntry
7	  - You want to understand registration mode (full vs setup vs CLI metadata)
8	  - You are looking up entry point options
9	---
10	
11	# Plugin Entry Points
12	
13	Every plugin exports a default entry object. The SDK provides three helpers for
14	creating them.
15	
16	<Tip>
17	  **Looking for a walkthrough?** See [Channel Plugins](/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins)
18	  or [Provider Plugins](/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins) for step-by-step guides.
19	</Tip>
20	
21	## `definePluginEntry`
22	
23	**Import:** `openclaw/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry`
24	
25	For provider plugins, tool plugins, hook plugins, and anything that is **not**
26	a messaging channel.
27	
28	```typescript
29	import { definePluginEntry } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry";
30	
31	export default definePluginEntry({
32	  id: "my-plugin",
33	  name: "My Plugin",
34	  description: "Short summary",
35	  register(api) {
36	    api.registerProvider({
37	      /* ... */
38	    });
39	    api.registerTool({
40	      /* ... */
41	    });
42	  },
43	});
44	```
45	
46	| Field          | Type                                                             | Required | Default             |
47	| -------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | -------- | ------------------- |
48	| `id`           | `string`                                                         | Yes      | —                   |
49	| `name`         | `string`                                                         | Yes      | —                   |
50	| `description`  | `string`                                                         | Yes      | —                   |
51	| `kind`         | `string`                                                         | No       | —                   |
52	| `configSchema` | `OpenClawPluginConfigSchema \| () => OpenClawPluginConfigSchema` | No       | Empty object schema |
53	| `register`     | `(api: OpenClawPluginApi) => void`                               | Yes      | —                   |
54	
55	- `id` must match your `openclaw.plugin.json` manifest.
56	- `kind` is for exclusive slots: `"memory"` or `"context-engine"`.
57	- `configSchema` can be a function for lazy evaluation.
58	
59	## `defineChannelPluginEntry`
60	
61	**Import:** `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`
62	
63	Wraps `definePluginEntry` with channel-specific wiring. Automatically calls
64	`api.registerChannel({ plugin })`, exposes an optional root-help CLI metadata
65	seam, and gates `registerFull` on registration mode.
66	
67	```typescript
68	import { defineChannelPluginEntry } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core";
69	
70	export default defineChannelPluginEntry({
71	  id: "my-channel",
72	  name: "My Channel",
73	  description: "Short summary",
74	  plugin: myChannelPlugin,
75	  setRuntime: setMyRuntime,
76	  registerCliMetadata(api) {
77	    api.registerCli(/* ... */);
78	  },
79	  registerFull(api) {
80	    api.registerGatewayMethod(/* ... */);
81	  },
82	});
83	```
84	
85	| Field                 | Type                                                             | Required | Default             |
86	| --------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | -------- | ------------------- |
87	| `id`                  | `string`                                                         | Yes      | —                   |
88	| `name`                | `string`                                                         | Yes      | —                   |
89	| `description`         | `string`                                                         | Yes      | —                   |
90	| `plugin`              | `ChannelPlugin`                                                  | Yes      | —                   |
91	| `configSchema`        | `OpenClawPluginConfigSchema \| () => OpenClawPluginConfigSchema` | No       | Empty object schema |
92	| `setRuntime`          | `(runtime: PluginRuntime) => void`                               | No       | —                   |
93	| `registerCliMetadata` | `(api: OpenClawPluginApi) => void`                               | No       | —                   |
94	| `registerFull`        | `(api: OpenClawPluginApi) => void`                               | No       | —                   |
95	
96	- `setRuntime` is called during registration so you can store the runtime reference
97	  (typically via `createPluginRuntimeStore`). It is skipped during CLI metadata
98	  capture.
99	- `registerCliMetadata` runs during both `api.registrationMode === "cli-metadata"`
100	  and `api.registrationMode === "full"`.
101	  Use it as the canonical place for channel-owned CLI descriptors so root help
102	  stays non-activating while normal CLI command registration remains compatible
103	  with full plugin loads.
104	- `registerFull` only runs when `api.registrationMode === "full"`. It is skipped
105	  during setup-only loading.
106	- For plugin-owned root CLI commands, prefer `api.registerCli(..., { descriptors: [...] })`
107	  when you want the command to stay lazy-loaded without disappearing from the
108	  root CLI parse tree. For channel plugins, prefer registering those descriptors
109	  from `registerCliMetadata(...)` and keep `registerFull(...)` focused on runtime-only work.
110	
111	## `defineSetupPluginEntry`
112	
113	**Import:** `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`
114	
115	For the lightweight `setup-entry.ts` file. Returns just `{ plugin }` with no
116	runtime or CLI wiring.
117	
118	```typescript
119	import { defineSetupPluginEntry } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core";
120	
121	export default defineSetupPluginEntry(myChannelPlugin);
122	```
123	
124	OpenClaw loads this instead of the full entry when a channel is disabled,
125	unconfigured, or when deferred loading is enabled. See
126	[Setup and Config](/plugins/sdk-setup#setup-entry) for when this matters.
127	
128	## Registration mode
129	
130	`api.registrationMode` tells your plugin how it was loaded:
131	
132	| Mode              | When                              | What to register              |
133	| ----------------- | --------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
134	| `"full"`          | Normal gateway startup            | Everything                    |
135	| `"setup-only"`    | Disabled/unconfigured channel     | Channel registration only     |
136	| `"setup-runtime"` | Setup flow with runtime available | Channel + lightweight runtime |
137	| `"cli-metadata"`  | Root help / CLI metadata capture  | CLI descriptors only          |
138	
139	`defineChannelPluginEntry` handles this split automatically. If you use
140	`definePluginEntry` directly for a channel, check mode yourself:
141	
142	```typescript
143	register(api) {
144	  if (api.registrationMode === "cli-metadata" || api.registrationMode === "full") {
145	    api.registerCli(/* ... */);
146	    if (api.registrationMode === "cli-metadata") return;
147	  }
148	
149	  api.registerChannel({ plugin: myPlugin });
150	  if (api.registrationMode !== "full") return;
151	
152	  // Heavy runtime-only registrations
153	  api.registerService(/* ... */);
154	}
155	```
156	
157	For CLI registrars specifically:
158	
159	- use `descriptors` when the registrar owns one or more root commands and you
160	  want OpenClaw to lazy-load the real CLI module on first invocation
161	- make sure those descriptors cover every top-level command root exposed by the
162	  registrar
163	- use `commands` alone only for eager compatibility paths
164	
165	## Plugin shapes
166	
167	OpenClaw classifies loaded plugins by their registration behavior:
168	
169	| Shape                 | Description                                        |
170	| --------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
171	| **plain-capability**  | One capability type (e.g. provider-only)           |
172	| **hybrid-capability** | Multiple capability types (e.g. provider + speech) |
173	| **hook-only**         | Only hooks, no capabilities                        |
174	| **non-capability**    | Tools/commands/services but no capabilities        |
175	
176	Use `openclaw plugins inspect <id>` to see a plugin's shape.
177	
178	## Related
179	
180	- [SDK Overview](/plugins/sdk-overview) — registration API and subpath reference
181	- [Runtime Helpers](/plugins/sdk-runtime) — `api.runtime` and `createPluginRuntimeStore`
182	- [Setup and Config](/plugins/sdk-setup) — manifest, setup entry, deferred loading
183	- [Channel Plugins](/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins) — building the `ChannelPlugin` object
184	- [Provider Plugins](/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins) — provider registration and hooks
185	

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

## Summary: OpenClaw Plugin SDK Public Contracts and Channel Plugin Invariants

Based on my thorough exploration of the OpenClaw codebase, here's the complete breakdown of plugin SDK contracts and requirements:

### 1. **Plugin Manifest (`openclaw.plugin.json`) - Required Fields**

All plugins must include:
- **`id`** (required, string): Canonical plugin identifier used in `plugins.entries.<id>`
- **`configSchema`** (required, object): Inline JSON Schema for the plugin's config with `type`, `additionalProperties`, and `properties`

For channel plugins specifically, add:
- **`channels`** (array): Channel IDs owned by this plugin (e.g., `["slack"]`)
- **`kind`** (optional): For exclusive slots, use `"channel"` (or `"memory"`, `"context-engine"` for others)

Optional fields (all plugins):
- `name`, `description`, `version`: Human-readable metadata
- `enabledByDefault`: Boolean to auto-enable bundled plugins (must be exactly `true`)
- `providers`, `cliBackends`: Related capability IDs
- `providerAuthEnvVars`, `providerAuthChoices`: Auth metadata
- `contracts`: Static bundled capability snapshots
- `uiHints`: Config field UI hints (labels, placeholders, sensitivity)

**Reference:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775467378370-m18w1n/docs/plugins/manifest.md`

### 2. **Channel Plugin Entry Point Export Contract**

Channel plugins must export from `index.ts`:

```typescript
import { defineChannelPluginEntry } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core";

export default defineChannelPluginEntry({
  id: "channel-id",           // Must match openclaw.plugin.json id
  name: "Display Name",
  description: "Short summary",
  plugin: myChannelPlugin,    // ChannelPlugin instance
  configSchema?: ...,         // Optional, defaults to empty schema
  setRuntime?: (runtime) => {}, // Called to store runtime reference
  registerCliMetadata?: (api) => {},  // CLI registration (runs in both modes)
  registerFull?: (api) => {}  // Full runtime registration (setup-only mode excluded)
});
```

**Setup Entry** (`setup-entry.ts` - optional but recommended):
```typescript
import { defineSetupPluginEntry } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core";
export default defineSetupPluginEntry(myChannelPlugin);
```

**Reference:** `/src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts`, `/src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`, `/docs/plugins/sdk-entrypoints.md`

### 3. **Channel ID Naming Conventions**

- **Built-in channels** (string union): `"telegram" | "whatsapp" | "discord" | "irc" | "googlechat" | "slack" | "signal" | "imessage" | "line"`
- **Type system**: `ChannelId = ChatChannelId | (string & {})` (allows both built-in and custom channel IDs)
- **No special naming requirements** beyond lowercase alphanumeric with hyphens (standard kebab-case)
- **Normalization**: Channel IDs are normalized case-insensitively for lookup

**Reference:** `/src/channels/ids.ts`, `/src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts`

### 4. **ChannelPlugin Interface - Required Properties**

At minimum, every `ChannelPlugin` must have:

```typescript
type ChannelPlugin = {
  id: ChannelId;                  // Required
  meta: ChannelMeta;              // Required
  config: ChannelConfigAdapter;   // Required
  setup?: ChannelSetupAdapter;    // Recommended
  // Optional adapters:
  capabilities?: ChannelCapabilities;
  setupWizard?: ChannelSetupWizardAdapter;
  configSchema?: ChannelConfigSchema;
  security?: ChannelSecurityAdapter;
  pairing?: ChannelPairingAdapter;
  outbound?: ChannelOutboundAdapter;
  threading?: ChannelThreadingAdapter;
  messaging?: ChannelMessagingAdapter;
  // ... more optional adapters
}
```

**Helpers:** Use `createChatChannelPlugin()` and `createChannelPluginBase()` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core` to build compliant plugins.

**Reference:** `/src/channels/plugins/types.plugin.ts`, `/src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`

### 5. **Text Chunk Limit Field**

The configuration field is called **`textChunkLimit`** (not `maxTokens`, not `chunkSize`):

```typescript
// In outbound config
textChunkLimit?: number;  // Character count per message chunk

// In config resolution (example from zalouser)
textChunkLimit: resolveZalouserOutboundTextChunkLimit(cfg, accountId)
```

This is typically set in:
- `channels.<channel-id>.textChunkLimit` config path
- Channel outbound adapter response objects
- Type: `number` (character count, not tokens)

**Examples:**
- Slack: default 4000 chars
- Discord: default 2000 chars
- Telegram: default 4000 chars

**Reference:** `/extensions/zalouser/src/types.ts`, `/extensions/*/openclaw.plugin.json`

### 6. **Plugin Invariant/Contract Tests**

Core test contracts for plugins:

**Package Manifest Contract** (`/src/plugins/contracts/package-manifest.contract.test.ts`):
- Validates runtime dependencies are plugin-local (not in root)
- Enforces `minHostVersion` floor (`openclaw.install.minHostVersion`) at baseline
- Tests for: bluebubbles, discord, feishu, googlechat, irc, line, matrix, mattermost, memory-lancedb, msteams, nextcloud-talk, nostr, slack, synology-chat, telegram, tlon, twitch, voice-call, whatsapp, zalo, zalouser

**Other Contract Suites:**
- `plugin-sdk-index.test.ts`: Entry point exports
- `plugin-registration.contract.test.ts`: Registration behavior
- `discovery.contract.test.ts`: Plugin discovery
- `provider.contract.test.ts`: Provider contracts
- Session binding contract validation (channels must register)

**Reference:** `/src/plugins/contracts/` directory (25+ contract test files)

### 7. **Package.json Structure for Channel Plugins**

```json
{
  "name": "@openclaw/<channel-id>",
  "version": "2026.4.1",
  "type": "module",
  "openclaw": {
    "extensions": ["./index.ts"],
    "setupEntry": "./setup-entry.ts",
    "channel": {
      "id": "channel-id",
      "label": "Display Name",
      "selectionLabel": "Optional longer label",
      "detailLabel": "Bot label",
      "docsPath": "/channels/<channel-id>",
      "blurb": "Status description",
      "systemImage": "SF Symbol name",
      "markdownCapable": true
    },
    "install": {
      "minHostVersion": ">=2026.4.1"
    },
    "bundle": {
      "stageRuntimeDependencies": true
    }
  },
  "dependencies": { /* runtime deps */ }
}
```

**Reference:** `/extensions/slack/package.json`, `/extensions/discord/package.json`

### 8. **Channel Plugin Examples**

Ready-made examples in the bundled workspace:
- **Slack** (`/extensions/slack/`): Full Socket Mode implementation with security/pairing
- **Discord** (`/extensions/discord/`): Comprehensive with approvals and message actions
- **Telegram** (`/extensions/telegram/`): Thread bindings and command gating
- **WhatsApp** (`/extensions/whatsapp/`): Account-scoped security
- **Matrix** (`/extensions/matrix/`): Native approval integration
- **Zalo/Zalouser** (`/extensions/zalo*/`): Multi-account patterns

All follow the pattern: `createChatChannelPlugin()` builder with optional security, pairing, threading, and outbound adapters.

**Reference:** `/extensions/*/src/channel.ts`, `/docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md` (step-by-step walkthrough)

### 9. **Plugin SDK Public Contracts**

The public SDK consists of:

**Entrypoints:** `/scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-entrypoints.json` (244 exports listed)

**Key Facade Subpaths:**
- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core` — Channel plugin helpers, `defineChannelPluginEntry`
- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry` — `definePluginEntry` for non-channel plugins
- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-contract` — Pure channel types (no implementations)
- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*/` — 240+ domain-specific helpers (approval-runtime, channel-config-helpers, text-chunking, etc.)

**Boundary Rules:**
- Import from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` only
- Do NOT import from `src/channels/**`, `src/agents/**`, `src/plugins/**`
- Keep plugins manifest-first (discovery/validation without code execution)

**Reference:** `/src/plugin-sdk/AGENTS.md`, `/scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-entrypoints.json`

### 10. **Bundled Plugin IDs (All Supported Channels)**

88 total plugins in `extensions/*/openclaw.plugin.json`:
- **Channels (21)**: bluebubbles, discord, feishu, googlechat, irc, imessage, line, matrix, mattermost, msteams, nextcloud-talk, nostr, slack, signal, synology-chat, telegram, tlon, twitch, whatsapp, zalo, zalouser
- **Providers (40+)**: openai, anthropic-vertex, deepseek, ollama, mistral, groq, xai, etc.
- **Memory/Context**: memory-core, memory-lancedb
- **Utilities**: device-pair, synthetic, voice-call, diagnostics-otel, etc.

**Reference:** Glob results show all 88 plugins in `/extensions/*/openclaw.plugin.json`

---

This covers all major SDK contracts, validation requirements, and field specifications needed to build a compliant new channel plugin.

## Comprehensive OpenClaw Channel Plugin Architecture Report

I've completed a thorough exploration of the OpenClaw channel plugin structure. Here's what I found:

### 1. BUNDLED WORKSPACE PLUGIN PACKAGES

**Total: 85 bundled plugins** in `/extensions/`

All plugins have the structure:
```
extensions/<plugin-id>/
├── openclaw.plugin.json      (manifest)
├── package.json              (npm metadata)
├── index.ts                  (main entry)
├── setup-entry.ts            (optional setup-only entry)
├── api.ts                    (public API barrel)
├── runtime-api.ts            (optional runtime facade)
└── src/                       (implementation)
```

Channel plugins specifically:
- `/extensions/irc/`
- `/extensions/nostr/`
- `/extensions/telegram/`
- Plus ~15 other chat channel plugins: discord, slack, matrix, mattermost, signal, whatsapp, line, feishu, googlechat, nextcloud-talk, synology-chat, tlon, qqbot, zalo, zalouser, bluebubbles, imessage

### 2. IRC, NOSTR, AND TELEGRAM PLUGIN DETAILS

#### IRC Plugin
**Manifest** (`openclaw.plugin.json`):
```json
{
  "id": "irc",
  "channels": ["irc"],
  "configSchema": { "type": "object", "additionalProperties": false, "properties": {} }
}
```

**Package.json Metadata**:
```json
{
  "name": "@openclaw/irc",
  "version": "2026.4.1-beta.1",
  "openclaw": {
    "extensions": ["./index.ts"],
    "setupEntry": "./setup-entry.ts",
    "channel": {
      "id": "irc",
      "label": "IRC",
      "selectionLabel": "IRC (Server + Nick)",
      "docsPath": "/channels/irc",
      "blurb": "classic IRC networks with DM/channel routing and pairing controls.",
      "systemImage": "network"
    }
  }
}
```

**Entry Point** (`index.ts`):
```typescript
export default defineChannelPluginEntry({
  id: "irc",
  name: "IRC",
  description: "IRC channel plugin",
```

### 5. BUNDLED PLUGIN TREE STRUCTURE GUIDANCE

From `/src/plugin-sdk/AGENTS.md` and `/extensions/CLAUDE.md`:

**Plugin SDK Boundary Rules**:
- Prefer narrow subpaths over broad re-exports
- Do not expose internals from `src/channels/**`, `src/agents/**`, `src/plugins/**`
- Prefer `api.runtime` or focused SDK facades
- When core/tests need bundled plugin helpers, expose via `api.ts` and `src/plugin-sdk/<id>.ts`

**Expanding Boundaries**:
- Keep changes additive and backwards-compatible
- Align: docs, `plugin-sdk-entrypoints.json`, `entrypoints.ts`, `package.json` exports, API baseline
- Update generated plugin facades instead of deep imports

**Extension Boundary Rules**:
- Import from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` and local `./api.ts`
- Do NOT import core internals or other extensions' `src/**`
- Do NOT use relative imports escaping package root
- Keep `openclaw.plugin.json` and package `openclaw` block accurate
- Export public surfaces via `api.ts` and optionally `src/plugin-sdk/<id>.ts` facades

### 6. KEY PATTERNS IN EXISTING PLUGINS

**IRC Plugin Pattern**:
```typescript
export const ircPlugin: ChannelPlugin<ResolvedIrcAccount, IrcProbe> = 
  createChatChannelPlugin({
    base: {
      id: "irc",
      meta: { ...getChatChannelMeta("irc"), quickstartAllowFrom: true },
      setup: ircSetupAdapter,
      setupWizard: ircSetupWizard,
      capabilities: { chatTypes: ["direct", "group"], media: true, blockStreaming: true },
      reload: { configPrefixes: ["channels.irc"] },
      configSchema: IrcChannelConfigSchema,
      config: { ...ircConfigAdapter, isConfigured, describeAccount },
      groups: { resolveRequireMention, resolveToolPolicy },
      messaging: { normalizeTarget, targetResolver },
      resolver: { resolveTargets },
      directory: createChannelDirectoryAdapter({ listPeers, listGroups }),
      status: createComputedAccountStatusAdapter({ ... }),
      gateway: { startAccount },
    },
    pairing: { text: { idLabel, message, notify } },
    security: { resolveDmPolicy: createScopedDmSecurityResolver(...), collectWarnings },
    outbound: {
      base: { deliveryMode: "direct", chunker, textChunkLimit: 350 },
      attachedResults: { channel: "irc", sendText, sendMedia }
    }
  });
```

**Telegram Plugin Pattern**:
- Uses `createChatChannelPlugin()` with extensive coverage
- Includes: actions, approvals, elevated mode, lifecycle, threads
- Handles 192+ source files of complex logic (bot framework, approvals, group policies)
- Entry registers message actions via `registerFull()` callback
- Uses branded helper `createTelegramPluginBase()` for shared setup

**Nostr Plugin Pattern**:
- Uses `createChatChannelPlugin()` with DM-only focus
- Minimal capabilities: `chatTypes: ["direct"], media: false`
- Custom `registerFull()` with HTTP route for profile management
- Account bus management (active buses per account map)
- Metrics snapshots per account for status reporting

### 7. PLUGIN SDK HELPER EXPORTS

Over **300+ helper files** in `/src/plugin-sdk/` organized by purpose:

**Channel-Specific**:
- `channel-config-helpers.ts` - scoped/top-level config adapters

- `channel-inbound.ts` - inbound envelope handling
- `directory-runtime.ts` - directory listing helpers
- `routing.ts` - session key building & route resolution
- `outbound-runtime.ts` - send dependency resolution

**Channel-Specific Facades** (for bundled plugins):
- `irc.ts` - IRC helpers
- `telegram-core.ts`, `telegram-account.ts`, `telegram-allow-from.ts`, `telegram-surface.ts`, `telegram-command-config.ts`

- `slack.ts`, `slack-core.ts`, `slack-account.ts`, etc.
- `matrix.ts`, `line.ts`, `signal.ts`, `whatsapp.ts` - similar pattern

**Shared Abstractions**:
- `extension-shared.ts` - `runStoppablePassiveMonitor()`, passive status builders
- `account-helpers.ts` - `describeAccountSnapshot()`
- `status-helpers.ts` - `createComputedAccountStatusAdapter()`, `createDefaultChannelRuntimeState()`
- `direct-dm.ts` - DM access and dispatch helpers
- `approval-*.ts` - approval infrastructure
- `reply-*.ts` - reply dispatch and chunking
- `text-chunking.ts` - message chunking utilities
- `command-*.ts` - command detection and auth
- `group-access.ts` - group policy resolution

### File Paths Summary

**Key Absolute Paths**:
- `/src/plugin-sdk/channel-contract.ts` - Channel types re-export
- `/src/plugin-sdk/core.ts` - Main SDK helpers (defineChannelPluginEntry, createChatChannelPlugin)
- `/src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts` - Generic plugin entry

```typescript
      setup: ircSetupAdapter,
      setupWizard: ircSetupWizard,
      capabilities: { chatTypes: ["direct", "group"], media: true, blockStreaming: true },
      reload: { configPrefixes: ["channels.irc"] },
      configSchema: IrcChannelConfigSchema,
      config: { ...ircConfigAdapter, isConfigured, describeAccount },
      groups: { resolveRequireMention, resolveToolPolicy },
      messaging: { normalizeTarget, targetResolver },
      resolver: { resolveTargets },
      directory: createChannelDirectoryAdapter({ listPeers, listGroups }),
      status: createComputedAccountStatusAdapter({ ... }),
      gateway: { startAccount },
    },
    pairing: { text: { idLabel, message, notify } },
    security: { resolveDmPolicy: createScopedDmSecurityResolver(...), collectWarnings },
    outbound: {
      base: { deliveryMode: "direct", chunker, textChunkLimit: 350 },
      attachedResults: { channel: "irc", sendText, sendMedia }
    }
  });
```

**Telegram Plugin Pattern**:
- Uses `createChatChannelPlugin()` with extensive coverage
- Includes: actions, approvals, elevated mode, lifecycle, threads
- Handles 192+ source files of complex logic (bot framework, approvals, group policies)
- Entry registers message actions via `registerFull()` callback
- Uses branded helper `createTelegramPluginBase()` for shared setup

**Nostr Plugin Pattern**:
- Uses `createChatChannelPlugin()` with DM-only focus
- Minimal capabilities: `chatTypes: ["direct"], media: false`
- Custom `registerFull()` with HTTP route for profile management
- Account bus management (active buses per account map)
- Metrics snapshots per account for status reporting

### 7. PLUGIN SDK HELPER EXPORTS

Over **300+ helper files** in `/src/plugin-sdk/` organized by purpose:

**Channel-Specific**:
- `channel-config-helpers.ts` - scoped/top-level config adapters
- `channel-policy.ts` - warning collectors, allowlist helpers
- `channel-pairing.ts` - pairing controller creation
- `channel-send-result.ts` - result attachment
- `channel-setup.ts` - setup adapter runtime
- `channel-status.ts` - status adapter runtime
- `channel-targets.ts` - target resolution
- `channel-inbound.ts` - inbound envelope handling
- `directory-runtime.ts` - directory listing helpers
- `routing.ts` - session key building & route resolution
- `outbound-runtime.ts` - send dependency resolution

**Channel-Specific Facades** (for bundled plugins):
- `irc.ts` - IRC helpers
- `telegram-core.ts`, `telegram-account.ts`, `telegram-allow-from.ts`, `telegram-surface.ts`, `telegram-command-config.ts`
- `discord.ts`, `discord-core.ts`, `discord-account.ts`, etc.
- `slack.ts`, `slack-core.ts`, `slack-account.ts`, etc.
- `matrix.ts`, `line.ts`, `signal.ts`, `whatsapp.ts` - similar pattern

**Shared Abstractions**:
- `extension-shared.ts` - `runStoppablePassiveMonitor()`, passive status builders
- `account-helpers.ts` - `describeAccountSnapshot()`
- `status-helpers.ts` - `createComputedAccountStatusAdapter()`, `createDefaultChannelRuntimeState()`
- `direct-dm.ts` - DM access and dispatch helpers
- `approval-*.ts` - approval infrastructure
- `reply-*.ts` - reply dispatch and chunking
- `text-chunking.ts` - message chunking utilities
- `command-*.ts` - command detection and auth
- `group-access.ts` - group policy resolution

### File Paths Summary

**Key Absolute Paths**:
- `/src/plugin-sdk/channel-contract.ts` - Channel types re-export
- `/src/plugin-sdk/core.ts` - Main SDK helpers (defineChannelPluginEntry, createChatChannelPlugin)
- `/src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts` - Generic plugin entry
- `/src/channels/plugins/types.plugin.ts` - ChannelPlugin type definition
- `/src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts` - Channel core types (meta, capabilities, snapshots, contexts)
- `/src/channels/plugins/types.adapters.ts` - Channel adapter types
- `/src/channels/AGENTS.md` - Channel boundary documentation
- `/src/plugin-sdk/AGENTS.md` - SDK boundary documentation
- `/extensions/irc/` - IRC plugin root
- `/extensions/telegram/` - Telegram plugin root
- `/extensions/nostr/` - Nostr plugin root
- `/extensions/*/openclaw.plugin.json` - 85 total bundled plugin manifests

This structure provides a comprehensive foundation for understanding how OpenClaw channel plugins are architected, registered, and implemented across the ecosystem.

9	
10	/**
11	 * Validates https:// URLs only (no javascript:, data:, file:, etc.)
12	 */
13	const safeUrlSchema = z
14	  .string()
15	  .url()
16	  .refine(
17	    (url) => {
18	      try {
19	        const parsed = new URL(url);
20	        return parsed.protocol === "https:";
21	      } catch {
22	        return false;
23	      }
24	    },
25	    { message: "URL must use https:// protocol" },
26	  );
27	
28	/**
29	 * NIP-01 profile metadata schema
30	 * https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/blob/master/01.md
31	 */
32	export const NostrProfileSchema = z.object({
33	  /** Username (NIP-01: name) - max 256 chars */
34	  name: z.string().max(256).optional(),
35	
36	  /** Display name (NIP-01: display_name) - max 256 chars */
37	  displayName: z.string().max(256).optional(),
38	
39	  /** Bio/description (NIP-01: about) - max 2000 chars */
40	  about: z.string().max(2000).optional(),
41	
42	  /** Profile picture URL (must be https) */
43	  picture: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
44	
45	  /** Banner image URL (must be https) */
46	  banner: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
47	
48	  /** Website URL (must be https) */
49	  website: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
50	
51	  /** NIP-05 identifier (e.g., "user@example.com") */
52	  nip05: z.string().optional(),
53	
54	  /** Lightning address (LUD-16) */
55	  lud16: z.string().optional(),
56	});
57	
58	export type NostrProfile = z.infer<typeof NostrProfileSchema>;
59	
60	/**
61	 * Zod schema for channels.nostr.* configuration
62	 */
63	export const NostrConfigSchema = z.object({
64	  /** Account name (optional display name) */
65	  name: z.string().optional(),
66	
67	  /** Optional default account id for routing/account selection. */
68	  defaultAccount: z.string().optional(),
69	
70	  /** Whether this channel is enabled */
71	  enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
72	
73	  /** Markdown formatting overrides (tables). */
74	  markdown: MarkdownConfigSchema,
75	
76	  /** Private key in hex or nsec bech32 format */
77	  privateKey: buildSecretInputSchema().optional(),
78	
79	  /** WebSocket relay URLs to connect to */
80	  relays: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
81	
82	  /** DM access policy: pairing, allowlist, open, or disabled */
83	  dmPolicy: DmPolicySchema.optional(),
84	
85	  /** Allowed sender pubkeys (npub or hex format) */
86	  allowFrom: AllowFromListSchema,
87	
88	  /** Profile metadata (NIP-01 kind:0 content) */
89	  profile: NostrProfileSchema.optional(),
90	});
91	
92	export type NostrConfig = z.infer<typeof NostrConfigSchema>;
93	
94	/**
95	 * JSON Schema for Control UI (converted from Zod)
96	 */
97	export const nostrChannelConfigSchema = buildChannelConfigSchema(NostrConfigSchema);

Now I have enough information to write a comprehensive plan. Let me now write the plan file.

Key decisions I've made:
1. PulseBoard will be modeled after the Nostr plugin (simpler) but with IRC-style group support since it needs both direct and group chat
2. It will use a top-level config adapter (single account) like Nostr
3. The channel ID is "pulseboard"
4. No external deps needed (it's fictional, so there's no real client library)
5. textChunkLimit = 4000 characters (as specified)


6. chatTypes will support both direct and group messaging

Now I'm mapping out the file structure I need to create for the plugin, with the main channel logic in one file, type definitions in another, and configuration schema handling in a third.

Actually, I realize the pnpm-workspace.yaml already auto-discovers anything under extensions/, so I don't need to modify it—the user was clear about only creating new files in the pulseboard directory.

Now I'm mapping out the plugin structure with the core files I'll need: the plugin manifest, package configuration, entry points for setup and API exposure, and then the implementation modules for channels, runtime, messaging, and monitoring. Setting up the plugin entry point and exports... I'm realizing there's a mismatch in how the runtime API should be structured for PulseBoard—Nostr has a dedicated SDK package it can export from, but PulseBoard doesn't have that same infrastructure, so I need to figure out what helpers and types should actually be exposed through the runtime-api barrel. Still writing export statements... Looking at the available SDK helpers across different subpaths, I can see there are many generic utilities beyond just the channel-specific facades—things like account helpers, channel configuration adapters, status management, routing constants, runtime stores, and schema builders that would all be available for PulseBoard to use.

Now I'm checking what Nostr actually imports from the runtime API to understand which of these exports are actually needed. - `formatPairingApproveHint`
- `resolveInboundDirectDmAccessWithRuntime`
- `type ChannelPlugin`

These all come from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/nostr` which is the shared re-export facade.

For PulseBoard, I need to create a similar barrel. Let me see what the plugin-sdk path would look like. Based on the exploration, the `scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-entrypoints.json` has 244 exports. There are subpaths like:
- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`
- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/nostr`
- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/irc`
- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-helpers`
- etc.

Looking at what the nostr extension actually imports from this facade, I can see it pulls in utilities like channel config schema builders, status issue collectors, DM authorization helpers, runtime state creators, account identifiers, dispatch functions for inbound messages, pairing hint formatters, and type definitions for the plugin contract and runtime.

Since I can't modify the plugin SDK itself to add a pulseboard subpath, the PulseBoard runtime-api will need to import directly from the individual SDK subpaths just like a third-party plugin would. I'm thinking through what PulseBoard specifically needs - things like the default account ID, a function to create the channel plugin, and likely some core utilities for handling messages and runtime state. I'm tracking down a few more imports I need to verify—`runStoppablePassiveMonitor`, the inbound dispatch functions, and the core types like `ChannelPlugin` and `PluginRuntime`. I'm wondering if some of these might be re-exported through the Nostr SDK facade, but I need to be careful about the self-import guardrail since PulseBoard is a separate extension and should be able to pull from there.

Looking at how the Nostr channel implementation handles this, it imports directly from specific SDK subpaths like account-helpers, channel-config-helpers, channel-pairing, and core rather than relying on a single facade import.

Setting up the imports... I'm seeing how the pattern works—I can set up direct SDK subpath imports in the specific files like `channel.ts` and `setup-surface.ts`, then have `runtime-api.ts` act as a barrel that re-exports the runtime-specific helpers. I need to figure out which SDK subpaths contain these helpers, and while I don't have the full entrypoints file, I know from the Explore agent that there are subpaths for things like channel config schemas, routing utilities, and account ID handling scattered across the SDK.

Actually, I think I have enough info to write the plan directly. Let me do that.

Here's my plan:

## PulseBoard Channel Plugin Implementation Plan

### Overview
PulseBoard is a fictional real-time bulletin board messaging platform that supports:
- **Direct messages (DM)** between users (user ID-based)
- **Group messages** via named "boards" (board name-based)
- **4000-character text chunk limit**
- Follows the exact same structure as IRC (for group support) and Nostr (for simplicity)


### File Structure
The plugin lives under `extensions/pulseboard/` with a manifest file, npm configuration, and TypeScript entry points for both the channel plugin and setup-only mode, along with API barrels that re-export the SDK runtime functionality and a `src/` directory for the channel implementation.

The channel subdirectory contains the core plugin definition using `createChatChannelPlugin`, a fictional PulseBoard client for connection and message handling, Zod schemas for configuration validation, handlers for inbound messages from DMs and board posts, a gateway monitoring loop, a runtime store for managing plugin state, outbound message sending for text and media, setup UI components including a wizard and adapter, and type definitions for account configuration and message structures.

I'm settling on a few key design choices: using a single implicit account with top-level channel config, supporting both direct messages and group board messaging with a 4000-character text limit, sending messages directly without needing a gateway, securing DMs through pairing by default with optional allowlists, keeping group boards open by default with optional per-board restrictions similar to IRC, and avoiding external dependencies since the client is fictional.

Now I'm defining the configuration schema with fields for the API token, server URL, bot username, DM and group policies, allowlists, and per-board settings.

Setting up the SDK imports I'll need from the plugin framework... Continuing through the plugin SDK modules, I'm seeing helpers for channel configuration, account snapshots, secret input validation, core plugin creation, runtime storage, and setup utilities.

For group message dispatch, I need to find `dispatchInboundReplyWithBase`. It's in the IRC-specific SDK path rather than the generic helpers, and looking at how `channel-inbound.ts` handles direct messages versus how IRC's `inbound.ts` handles group replies, they use different dispatch functions from the runtime API layer. I need to figure out the right dispatch function for PulseBoard's group channels since `dispatchInboundDirectDmWithRuntime` is specifically for direct messages. Let me look at how the Nostr plugin handles this to see if there's a pattern for group-based channels. Looking at how the dispatch functions work, I see that `dispatchInboundDirectDmWithRuntime` is specifically designed for direct messages, while `dispatchInboundReplyWithBase` is the more general function that handles both DMs and group messages by accepting different peer configurations. For PulseBoard to support groups properly, I should use the lower-level `dispatchInboundReplyWithBase` approach with the appropriate peer kind and group ID, similar to how IRC handles it. Looking at how `dispatchInboundDirectDmWithRuntime` is actually used in the codebase, it seems to handle both direct messages and group messages based on the `peer.kind` field, despite the function name suggesting it's only for direct DMs.

For PulseBoard, I could either use that same higher-level dispatch function for both cases if it truly supports groups, or follow IRC's pattern of building the context payload manually for groups while using the dispatch function for direct messages.

Let me look at the actual function signature and type definitions in `direct-dm.ts` to see whether it's truly limited to DMs or if it can handle group contexts.

I see that `dispatchInboundDirectDmWithRuntime` takes a `DirectDmRoutePeer` with `kind: "direct"` only (not group). So for group messages, we'd need a different approach.

For PulseBoard's group board handling, I have two options:

1. **Use `dispatchInboundDirectDmWithRuntime` for DMs only**, and for group boards use a simplified approach that builds the context manually (like IRC does with `dispatchInboundReplyWithBase`).

2. **Simplify the group implementation** - since PulseBoard is fictional, we can model boards as having the same API as DMs (treating the board name as a "user" in terms of routing). This lets us use `dispatchInboundDirectDmWithRuntime` for both DMs and board messages, with `peer.kind: "direct"` for DMs and... 

But the type signature enforces `kind: "direct"` as a hardcoded literal, so I can't repurpose it for group messages. Let me check if the exported function has a more flexible peer type than what the internal type definition shows.

Now I see that `dispatchInboundDirectDmWithRuntime` has `peer: DirectDmRoutePeer` which is typed as `{ kind: "direct"; id: string }`. But looking at the actual function implementation (line 254-313), the `peer` is only passed to `resolveInboundRouteEnvelopeBuilderWithRuntime` and the result used to create the route. The `ChatType` is hardcoded to `"direct"` at line 279.

So this function is DM-ONLY and can't handle group board messages properly.

For PulseBoard's group board support, I have a few approaches:


Option A won't work cleanly because `dispatchInboundReplyWithBase` is only available through specific SDK paths like IRC and Nextcloud Talk, not as a generic utility, and I can't add new SDK paths. input.summary:
## PulseBoard Channel Plugin Plan

### What I found
- All bundled channel plugins live in `extensions/<id>/` — the `pnpm-workspace.yaml` already globs `extensions/*`, so no existing files need touching
- **Nostr** is the closest model (simple, single account, top-level config, `dispatchInboundDirectDmWithRuntime`) — used as the primary template
- **IRC** provides the group/board dispatch pattern and `runStoppablePassiveMonitor`
- A key SDK detail: `dispatchInboundDirectDmWithRuntime` accepts `extraContext` that is spread *after* `ChatType: "direct"` in `finalizeInboundContext`, enabling `extraContext: { ChatType: "group", GroupSubject: boardName }` to correctly mark board messages as group chats — no SDK modifications needed

### 15 new files to create under `extensions/pulseboard/`

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `openclaw.plugin.json` | Manifest with `"id": "pulseboard"`, `"channels": ["pulseboard"]` |
| `package.json` | `@openclaw/pulseboard` with `openclaw.channel` block, no external deps |
| `index.ts` | `defineChannelPluginEntry` entry point |
| `setup-entry.ts` | `defineSetupPluginEntry` for setup-only mode |
| `api.ts` | Public barrel (re-exports `runtime-api.ts`) |
| `runtime-api.ts` | Private barrel re-exporting from granular SDK subpaths (no dedicated `openclaw/plugin-sdk/pulseboard` needed) |
| `src/types.ts` | `PulseBoardAccountConfig`, `ResolvedPulseBoardAccount`, `PulseBoardInboundMessage`, `CoreConfig` |
| `src/config-schema.ts` | Zod schema: `token`, `serverUrl`, `username`, `dmPolicy`, `allowFrom`, `groupPolicy`, `boards.<name>.*` |
| `src/runtime.ts` | `createPluginRuntimeStore<PluginRuntime>` |
| `src/client.ts` | Fictional WebSocket client stub (`connectPulseBoardClient`, `PulseBoardClientHandle`) |
| `src/send.ts` | `sendMessagePulseBoard` — routes to `client.sendDm` or `client.postToBoard` |
| `src/inbound.ts` | `handlePulseBoardInbound` — DM path uses standard access resolution; board path uses `extraContext` override |
| `src/monitor.ts` | `monitorPulseBoardProvider` — gateway loop via `runStoppablePassiveMonitor` |
| `src/setup-surface.ts` | `pulseBoardSetupAdapter` + `pulseBoardSetupWizard` (token, serverUrl, username, dmPolicy wizard) |
| `src/channel.ts` | `createChatChannelPlugin` with `chatTypes: ["direct", "group"]`, `textChunkLimit: 4000`, `deliveryMode: "direct"` |

The plan is ready for your review at `/Users/timpietrusky/.claude/plans/humble-napping-oasis.md`. Here's a summary:

## PulseBoard Channel Plugin — Plan Summary

### 15 new files under `extensions/pulseboard/` (no existing files modified)

**Root level (6 files):**
| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `openclaw.plugin.json` | Manifest: `"id": "pulseboard"`, `"channels": ["pulseboard"]` |
| `package.json` | `@openclaw/pulseboard`, no external npm deps |
| `index.ts` | `defineChannelPluginEntry` — plugin entry point |
| `setup-entry.ts` | `defineSetupPluginEntry` — setup-only mode |
| `api.ts` | Public barrel (re-exports `runtime-api.ts`) |
| `runtime-api.ts` | Private barrel re-exporting from granular SDK subpaths (e.g. `channel-inbound`, `status-helpers`, `extension-shared`, etc.) |

**`src/` (9 files):**
| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `types.ts` | `ResolvedPulseBoardAccount`, `PulseBoardInboundMessage`, `CoreConfig` |
| `config-schema.ts` | Zod schema: `token`, `serverUrl`, `username`, `dmPolicy`, `allowFrom`, `boards.*` |
| `runtime.ts` | `createPluginRuntimeStore<PluginRuntime>` |
| `client.ts` | Fictional WebSocket client stub (`connectPulseBoardClient`) |
| `send.ts` | `sendMessagePulseBoard` — routes to `sendDm` or `postToBoard` |
| `inbound.ts` | `handlePulseBoardInbound` — DM + board dispatch |
| `monitor.ts` | `monitorPulseBoardProvider` — gateway loop via `runStoppablePassiveMonitor` |
| `setup-surface.ts` | `pulseBoardSetupAdapter` + wizard (token, serverUrl, dmPolicy) |
| `channel.ts` | `createChatChannelPlugin` — `chatTypes: ["direct","group"]`, `textChunkLimit: 4000` |

### Key design decision
For group board messages, `dispatchInboundDirectDmWithRuntime` is reused with `extraContext: { ChatType: "group", GroupSubject: boardName }` — this works because `extraContext` is spread *after* the hardcoded `ChatType: "direct"` inside the function, overriding it. Board peer IDs are namespaced as `board:<name>` to prevent session key collisions with same-named DM users.
